"""Resolve selected papers to arXiv and download PDFs into papers/."""
import re, time, json, sys
import arxiv

# (filename_stem, search_title) for the papers selected for this review.
SELECTED = [
    ("emergent_response_planning", "Emergent Response Planning in LLMs"),
    ("reasoning_strength_planning", "On Reasoning Strength Planning in Large Reasoning Models"),
    ("do_lms_plan_ahead", "Do language models plan ahead for future tokens"),
    ("future_lens", "Future Lens: Anticipating Subsequent Tokens from a Single Hidden State"),
    ("detecting_planning_lms", "Detecting and Characterizing Planning in Language Models"),
    ("internal_planning_horizon_branch", "Internal Planning in Language Models: Characterizing Horizon and Branch Awareness"),
    ("whats_the_plan_implicit_planning", "What's the plan? Metrics for implicit planning in LLMs and their application to rhyme generation"),
    ("try_again_multiturn", "A Simple Try Again Can Elicit Multi-Turn LLM Reasoning"),
    ("score_self_correct_rl", "Training Language Models to Self-Correct via Reinforcement Learning"),
    ("recursive_introspection", "Recursive Introspection: Teaching Language Model Agents How to Self-Improve"),
    ("flipflop_are_you_sure", "Are You Sure? Challenging LLMs Leads to Performance Drops in The FlipFlop Experiment"),
    ("truth_decay_sycophancy", "TRUTH DECAY: Quantifying Multi-Turn Sycophancy in Language Models"),
    ("overconfidence_changing_minds", "How Overconfidence in Initial Choices and Underconfidence Under Criticism Modulate Changing Minds in LLMs"),
    ("measuring_sycophancy_multiturn", "Measuring Sycophancy of Language Models in Multi-turn Dialogues"),
    ("cot_uq", "CoT-UQ: Improving Response-wise Uncertainty Quantification in LLMs with Chain-of-Thought"),
    ("lost_in_multiturn_curriculum_rl", "Mitigating Lost in Multi-turn Conversation via Curriculum RL"),
    ("not_all_thoughts_equal", "Not All Thoughts are Generated Equal: Efficient LLM Reasoning via Multi-Turn Reinforcement Learning"),
    ("uncertainty_profiles_decomposition", "Uncertainty Profiles for LLMs: Uncertainty Source Decomposition"),
]

client = arxiv.Client(page_size=5, delay_seconds=3, num_retries=3)

def norm(s):
    return re.sub(r'[^a-z0-9]', '', s.lower())

results_log = []
for stem, title in SELECTED:
    try:
        q = re.sub(r'[^a-zA-Z0-9 ]', ' ', title)
        search = arxiv.Search(query=q, max_results=5,
                              sort_by=arxiv.SortCriterion.Relevance)
        best = None
        nt = norm(title)
        for r in client.results(search):
            rt = norm(r.title)
            # token overlap score
            a = set(norm(title[:60]))  # crude
            # better: word overlap
            tw = set(re.findall(r'[a-z0-9]+', title.lower()))
            rw = set(re.findall(r'[a-z0-9]+', r.title.lower()))
            overlap = len(tw & rw) / max(1, len(tw))
            if best is None or overlap > best[0]:
                best = (overlap, r)
        if best and best[0] >= 0.5:
            r = best[1]
            aid = r.get_short_id()
            path = f"papers/{stem}.pdf"
            r.download_pdf(dirpath="papers", filename=f"{stem}.pdf")
            results_log.append({"stem": stem, "title": title, "matched": r.title,
                                "arxiv_id": aid, "overlap": round(best[0],2),
                                "url": r.entry_id, "status": "ok"})
            print(f"OK   {stem:38s} <- {aid}  ({best[0]:.2f}) {r.title[:60]}")
        else:
            ov = best[0] if best else 0
            results_log.append({"stem": stem, "title": title, "status": "no_match",
                                "best_overlap": round(ov,2),
                                "best_title": best[1].title if best else None})
            print(f"MISS {stem:38s} best overlap {ov:.2f} : {best[1].title[:50] if best else 'none'}")
    except Exception as e:
        results_log.append({"stem": stem, "title": title, "status": "error", "error": str(e)})
        print(f"ERR  {stem:38s} {e}")
    time.sleep(1)

json.dump(results_log, open("papers/_download_log.json", "w"), indent=2)
print("\n=== summary ===")
ok = sum(1 for r in results_log if r['status']=='ok')
print(f"downloaded {ok}/{len(SELECTED)}")

input.content:
"""Resolve selected papers to arXiv and download PDFs into papers/."""
import re, time, json
import arxiv, requests

SELECTED = [
    ("emergent_response_planning", "Emergent Response Planning in LLMs"),
    ("reasoning_strength_planning", "On Reasoning Strength Planning in Large Reasoning Models"),
    ("do_lms_plan_ahead", "Do language models plan ahead for future tokens"),
    ("future_lens", "Future Lens Anticipating Subsequent Tokens from a Single Hidden State"),
    ("detecting_planning_lms", "Detecting and Characterizing Planning in Language Models"),
    ("internal_planning_horizon_branch", "Internal Planning in Language Models Characterizing Horizon and Branch Awareness"),
    ("whats_the_plan_implicit_planning", "What's the plan Metrics for implicit planning in language models rhyme generation"),
    ("try_again_multiturn", "A Simple Try Again Can Elicit Multi-Turn LLM Reasoning"),
    ("score_self_correct_rl", "Training Language Models to Self-Correct via Reinforcement Learning"),
    ("recursive_introspection", "Recursive Introspection Teaching Language Model Agents How to Self-Improve"),
    ("flipflop_are_you_sure", "Are You Sure Challenging LLMs Leads to Performance Drops in The FlipFlop Experiment"),
    ("truth_decay_sycophancy", "Truth Decay Quantifying Multi-Turn Sycophancy in Language Models"),
    ("overconfidence_changing_minds", "Overconfidence in Initial Choices and Underconfidence Under Criticism Modulate Changing Minds in LLMs"),
    ("measuring_sycophancy_multiturn", "Measuring Sycophancy of Language Models in Multi-turn Dialogues"),
    ("cot_uq", "CoT-UQ Improving Response-wise Uncertainty Quantification in LLMs with Chain-of-Thought"),
    ("lost_in_multiturn_curriculum_rl", "Mitigating Lost in Multi-turn Conversation via Curriculum Reinforcement Learning"),
    ("not_all_thoughts_equal", "Not All Thoughts are Generated Equal Efficient LLM Reasoning Multi-Turn Reinforcement Learning"),
    ("uncertainty_profiles_decomposition", "Uncertainty Profiles for LLMs Uncertainty Source Decomposition"),
]

client = arxiv.Client(page_size=8, delay_seconds=3, num_retries=3)
HDRS = {"User-Agent": "Mozilla/5.0 (research paper fetcher)"}

def words(s):
    return set(re.findall(r'[a-z0-9]+', s.lower()))

log = []
for stem, title in SELECTED:
    try:
        q = re.sub(r'[^a-zA-Z0-9 ]', ' ', title)
        search = arxiv.Search(query=q, max_results=8,
                              sort_by=arxiv.SortCriterion.Relevance)
        tw = words(title)
        best = None
        for r in client.results(search):
            ov = len(tw & words(r.title)) / max(1, len(tw))
            if best is None or ov > best[0]:
                best = (ov, r)
        if best and best[0] >= 0.55:
            r = best[1]
            aid = r.get_short_id()
            url = r.pdf_url
            resp = requests.get(url, headers=HDRS, timeout=60)
            resp.raise_for_status()
            with open(f"papers/{stem}.pdf", "wb") as f:
                f.write(resp.content)
            kb = len(resp.content)//1024
            log.append({"stem": stem, "title": title, "matched": r.title,
                        "arxiv_id": aid, "overlap": round(best[0],2),
                        "pdf_url": url, "abs_url": r.entry_id, "kb": kb, "status": "ok"})
            print(f"OK   {stem:36s} {aid:13s} {kb:5d}KB ov={best[0]:.2f} {r.title[:50]}")
        else:
            ov = best[0] if best else 0
            log.append({"stem": stem, "title": title, "status": "no_match",
                        "best_overlap": round(ov,2),
                        "best_title": best[1].title if best else None})
            print(f"MISS {stem:36s} ov={ov:.2f} : {best[1].title[:55] if best else 'none'}")
    except Exception as e:
        log.append({"stem": stem, "title": title, "status": "error", "error": str(e)})
        print(f"ERR  {stem:36s} {e}")
    time.sleep(1)

json.dump(log, open("papers/_download_log.json", "w"), indent=2)
ok = sum(1 for r in log if r['status']=='ok')
print(f"\n=== downloaded {ok}/{len(SELECTED)} ===")

    try:
        q = re.sub(r'[^a-zA-Z0-9 ]', ' ', title)
        search = arxiv.Search(query=q, max_results=8,
                              sort_by=arxiv.SortCriterion.Relevance)
        tw = words(title)
        best = None
        for r in client.results(search):
            ov = len(tw & words(r.title)) / max(1, len(tw))
            if best is None or ov > best[0]:
                best = (ov, r)
        if best and best[0] >= 0.55:
            r = best[1]
            aid = r.get_short_id()
            url = r.pdf_url
            resp = requests.get(url, headers=HDRS, timeout=60)
            resp.raise_for_status()
            with open(f"papers/{stem}.pdf", "wb") as f:
                f.write(resp.content)
            kb = len(resp.content)//1024
            log.append({"stem": stem, "title": title, "matched": r.title,
                        "arxiv_id": aid, "overlap": round(best[0],2),
                        "pdf_url": url, "abs_url": r.entry_id, "kb": kb, "status": "ok"})
            print(f"OK   {stem:36s} {aid:13s} {kb:5d}KB ov={best[0]:.2f} {r.title[:50]}")
        else:
            ov = best[0] if best else 0
            log.append({"stem": stem, "title": title, "status": "no_match",
                        "best_overlap": round(ov,2),
                        "best_title": best[1].title if best else None})
            print(f"MISS {stem:36s} ov={ov:.2f} : {best[1].title[:55] if best else 'none'}")
    except Exception as e:
        log.append({"stem": stem, "title": title, "status": "error", "error": str(e)})
        print(f"ERR  {stem:36s} {e}")
    time.sleep(1)

json.dump(log, open("papers/_download_log.json", "w"), indent=2)
ok = sum(1 for r in log if r['status']=='ok')
print(f"\n=== downloaded {ok}/{len(SELECTED)} ===")

tool_use_result.originalFile:
"""Resolve selected papers to arXiv and download PDFs into papers/."""
import re, time, json, sys
import arxiv

# (filename_stem, search_title) for the papers selected for this review.
SELECTED = [
    ("emergent_response_planning", "Emergent Response Planning in LLMs"),
    ("reasoning_strength_planning", "On Reasoning Strength Planning in Large Reasoning Models"),
    ("do_lms_plan_ahead", "Do language models plan ahead for future tokens"),
    ("future_lens", "Future Lens: Anticipating Subsequent Tokens from a Single Hidden State"),
    ("detecting_planning_lms", "Detecting and Characterizing Planning in Language Models"),
    ("internal_planning_horizon_branch", "Internal Planning in Language Models: Characterizing Horizon and Branch Awareness"),
    ("whats_the_plan_implicit_planning", "What's the plan? Metrics for implicit planning in LLMs and their application to rhyme generation"),
    ("try_again_multiturn", "A Simple Try Again Can Elicit Multi-Turn LLM Reasoning"),
    ("score_self_correct_rl", "Training Language Models to Self-Correct via Reinforcement Learning"),
    ("recursive_introspection", "Recursive Introspection: Teaching Language Model Agents How to Self-Improve"),
    ("flipflop_are_you_sure", "Are You Sure? Challenging LLMs Leads to Performance Drops in The FlipFlop Experiment"),
    ("truth_decay_sycophancy", "TRUTH DECAY: Quantifying Multi-Turn Sycophancy in Language Models"),
    ("overconfidence_changing_minds", "How Overconfidence in Initial Choices and Underconfidence Under Criticism Modulate Changing Minds in LLMs"),
    ("measuring_sycophancy_multiturn", "Measuring Sycophancy of Language Models in Multi-turn Dialogues"),
    ("cot_uq", "CoT-UQ: Improving Response-wise Uncertainty Quantification in LLMs with Chain-of-Thought"),
    ("lost_in_multiturn_curriculum_rl", "Mitigating Lost in Multi-turn Conversation via Curriculum RL"),
    ("not_all_thoughts_equal", "Not All Thoughts are Generated Equal: Efficient LLM Reasoning via Multi-Turn Reinforcement Learning"),
    ("uncertainty_profiles_decomposition", "Uncertainty Profiles for LLMs: Uncertainty Source Decomposition"),
]

client = arxiv.Client(page_size=5, delay_seconds=3, num_retries=3)

def norm(s):
    return re.sub(r'[^a-z0-9]', '', s.lower())

results_log = []
for stem, title in SELECTED:
    try:
        q = re.sub(r'[^a-zA-Z0-9 ]', ' ', title)
        search = arxiv.Search(query=q, max_results=5,
                              sort_by=arxiv.SortCriterion.Relevance)
        best = None
        nt = norm(title)
        for r in client.results(search):
            rt = norm(r.title)
            # token overlap score
            a = set(norm(title[:60]))  # crude
            # better: word overlap
            tw = set(re.findall(r'[a-z0-9]+', title.lower()))
            rw = set(re.findall(r'[a-z0-9]+', r.title.lower()))
            overlap = len(tw & rw) / max(1, len(tw))
            if best is None or overlap > best[0]:
                best = (overlap, r)
        if best and best[0] >= 0.5:
            r = best[1]
            aid = r.get_short_id()
            path = f"papers/{stem}.pdf"
            r.download_pdf(dirpath="papers", filename=f"{stem}.pdf")
            results_log.append({"stem": stem, "title": title, "matched": r.title,
                                "arxiv_id": aid, "overlap": round(best[0],2),
                                "url": r.entry_id, "status": "ok"})
            print(f"OK   {stem:38s} <- {aid}  ({best[0]:.2f}) {r.title[:60]}")
        else:
            ov = best[0] if best else 0
            results_log.append({"stem": stem, "title": title, "status": "no_match",
                                "best_overlap": round(ov,2),
                                "best_title": best[1].title if best else None})
            print(f"MISS {stem:38s} best overlap {ov:.2f} : {best[1].title[:50] if best else 'none'}")
    except Exception as e:
        results_log.append({"stem": stem, "title": title, "status": "error", "error": str(e)})
        print(f"ERR  {stem:38s} {e}")
    time.sleep(1)

json.dump(results_log, open("papers/_download_log.json", "w"), indent=2)
print("\n=== summary ===")
ok = sum(1 for r in results_log if r['status']=='ok')
print(f"downloaded {ok}/{len(SELECTED)}")

input.command:
source .venv/bin/activate && python -c "
import arxiv, requests, json
c=arxiv.Client(page_size=10,delay_seconds=3,num_retries=3)
HDRS={'User-Agent':'Mozilla/5.0 (research fetcher)'}
ids={
 '2506.08390':'reasoning_strength_planning',
 '2311.04897':'future_lens',
 '2508.18098':'detecting_planning_lms',
 '2601.20164':'whats_the_plan_implicit_planning',
 '2409.12917':'score_self_correct_rl',
 '2505.07309':'uncertainty_profiles_decomposition',
}
log=json.load(open('papers/_download_log.json'))
res=list(c.results(arxiv.Search(id_list=list(ids.keys()))))
for r in res:
    aid=r.get_short_id(); base=aid.split('v')[0]
    stem=ids.get(base) or ids.get(aid)
    rr=requests.get(r.pdf_url,headers=HDRS,timeout=90); rr.raise_for_status()
    open(f'papers/{stem}.pdf','wb').write(rr.content)
    log=[e for e in log if e['stem']!=stem]
    log.append({'stem':stem,'matched':r.title,'arxiv_id':aid,'pdf_url':r.pdf_url,'abs_url':r.entry_id,'kb':len(rr.content)//1024,'status':'ok'})
    print('OK',stem,aid,len(rr.content)//1024,'KB',r.title[:50])
# try again paper
for q in ['Unary Feedback as Observation reinforcement learning','\"Try Again\" multi-turn reasoning unary feedback']:
    s=arxiv.Search(query=q,max_results=6,sort_by=arxiv.SortCriterion.Relevance)
    hit=None
    for r in c.results(s):
        if 'try again' in r.title.lower() or 'unary feedback' in r.title.lower():
            hit=r;break
    if hit:
        rr=requests.get(hit.pdf_url,headers=HDRS,timeout=90);rr.raise_for_status()
        open('papers/try_again_multiturn.pdf','wb').write(rr.content)
        log=[e for e in log if e['stem']!='try_again_multiturn']
        log.append({'stem':'try_again_multiturn','matched':hit.title,'arxiv_id':hit.get_short_id(),'pdf_url':hit.pdf_url,'status':'ok'})
        print('OK try_again_multiturn',hit.get_short_id(),hit.title[:50]); break
else:
    print('try_again still missing')
json.dump(log,open('papers/_download_log.json','w'),indent=2)
print('total ok pdfs:',sum(1 for e in log if e['status']=='ok'))
"

content.text: Now I'll deep-read the 7 central papers in parallel via a workflow, with each agent reading all chunks of one paper and returning structured notes tied to the hypothesis.

input.script:
export const meta = {
  name: 'deep-read-papers',
  description: 'Deep-read 7 central papers in parallel, extract structured notes tied to the research hypothesis',
  phases: [{ title: 'DeepRead', detail: 'one agent per paper reads all chunks' }],
}

const HYP = `RESEARCH HYPOTHESIS: RL-fine-tuned LLMs tend to pre-plan the end of their next turn, giving them LESS ability to react to new information than base LLMs. By rotating what is certain about the answer across a series of questions, one might force the model to distribute its uncertainty across different parts of a larger question.`

const PAPERS = [
  { stem: 'emergent_response_planning', n: 7, title: 'Emergent Response Planning in LLMs' },
  { stem: 'reasoning_strength_planning', n: 10, title: 'On Reasoning Strength Planning in Large Reasoning Models' },
  { stem: 'do_lms_plan_ahead', n: 8, title: 'Do language models plan ahead for future tokens?' },
  { stem: 'flipflop_are_you_sure', n: 5, title: 'Are You Sure? Challenging LLMs (FlipFlop Experiment)' },
  { stem: 'try_again_multiturn', n: 11, title: 'A Simple "Try Again" Can Elicit Multi-Turn LLM Reasoning (UFO)' },
  { stem: 'score_self_correct_rl', n: 10, title: 'Training Language Models to Self-Correct via RL (SCoRe)' },
  { stem: 'detecting_planning_lms', n: 7, title: 'Detecting and Characterizing Planning in Language Models' },
]

const SCHEMA = {
  type: 'object',
  required: ['title','core_contribution','methodology','datasets','baselines','metrics','key_results','relevance_to_hypothesis','code_links','reusable_for_experiment'],
  properties: {
    title: { type: 'string' },
    core_contribution: { type: 'string', description: '2-3 sentences' },
    methodology: { type: 'string', description: 'detailed: methods, algorithms, experimental setup' },
    datasets: { type: 'string', description: 'names, sizes, sources' },
    baselines: { type: 'string' },
    metrics: { type: 'string' },
    key_results: { type: 'string', description: 'concrete numbers where available' },
    relevance_to_hypothesis: { type: 'string', description: 'how it supports/refutes/informs the specific hypothesis above; note evidence on RL vs base, answer pre-planning, reactivity to new info, or uncertainty' },
    code_links: { type: 'string', description: 'github/data URLs found in the paper, or "none found"' },
    reusable_for_experiment: { type: 'string', description: 'datasets/benchmarks/probing techniques/metrics we could reuse to TEST the hypothesis' },
  },
}

phase('DeepRead')
const notes = await parallel(PAPERS.map(p => () =>
  agent(
    `You are doing a DEEP read of one academic paper to support a research project.\n\n${HYP}\n\nThe paper "${p.title}" has been split into ${p.n} PDF chunks at:\n  papers/pages/${p.stem}_chunk_001.pdf ... ${p.stem}_chunk_${String(p.n).padStart(3,'0')}.pdf\n(working directory is /workspaces/llms-know-answer-73ae-claude). Read EVERY chunk PDF in order with the Read tool (they are small PDFs). Do not skip chunks — methodology and results are in the middle/later chunks. Then return structured notes. Be concrete and quantitative. Focus especially on anything about: (a) models internally planning/committing to an answer or response length before generating, (b) differences between RL-fine-tuned and base models, (c) failure to revise/react to new information or feedback, (d) uncertainty across question parts. Pay attention to exact dataset names, benchmark names, model names, and metrics we could reuse.`,
    { label: p.stem, phase: 'DeepRead', schema: SCHEMA }
  ).then(r => ({ stem: p.stem, ...r }))
))

return notes.filter(Boolean)

description: Deep-read 7 central papers in parallel, extract structured notes tied to the research hypothesis

content.text: The deep-read workflow is running in the background. Meanwhile, let me set up datasets. For testing this hypothesis we need benchmarks that are (a) decomposable into sub-questions (multi-hop QA), (b) clean multiple-choice for answer-commitment measurement, and (c) math reasoning where RL-vs-base gaps are well documented. Let me install the datasets library and pull samples.

input.command: source .venv/bin/activate && uv add datasets 2>&1 | tail -4

input.content:
"""Download small samples of benchmarks for testing the hypothesis.
Saves full small splits where cheap, otherwise streamed samples + sample JSON.
"""
import json, os, traceback
os.makedirs("datasets", exist_ok=True)
from datasets import load_dataset

def save_samples(name, recs, n=10):
    os.makedirs(f"datasets/{name}", exist_ok=True)
    with open(f"datasets/{name}/samples.json", "w") as f:
        json.dump(recs[:n], f, indent=2, default=str)
    print(f"  saved {len(recs)} recs -> datasets/{name}/ (sample of {min(n,len(recs))})")

report = {}

# 1. GSM8K - grade-school math, canonical RL-vs-base reasoning benchmark
try:
    ds = load_dataset("openai/gsm8k", "main", split="test")
    recs = [dict(ds[i]) for i in range(len(ds))]
    ds.to_json("datasets/gsm8k/test.jsonl")
    save_samples("gsm8k", recs)
    report["gsm8k"] = {"n": len(recs), "split": "test", "fields": list(recs[0].keys())}
except Exception as e:
    report["gsm8k"] = {"error": str(e)}; traceback.print_exc()

# 2. 2WikiMultihopQA-style multi-hop QA, naturally decomposable into sub-questions
try:
    ds = load_dataset("hotpotqa/hotpot_qa", "distractor", split="validation", trust_remote_code=True)
    recs = [{"id": ds[i]["id"], "question": ds[i]["question"], "answer": ds[i]["answer"],
             "type": ds[i].get("type"), "level": ds[i].get("level"),
             "supporting_facts": ds[i].get("supporting_facts")} for i in range(min(2000, len(ds)))]
    save_samples("hotpotqa", recs)
    with open("datasets/hotpotqa/sample2000.jsonl", "w") as f:
        for r in recs: f.write(json.dumps(r, default=str)+"\n")
    report["hotpotqa"] = {"n": len(recs), "split": "validation(sample)", "fields": list(recs[0].keys())}
except Exception as e:
    report["hotpotqa"] = {"error": str(e)}; traceback.print_exc()

# 3. ARC-Challenge multiple choice - clean for answer-commitment / FlipFlop paradigm
try:
    ds = load_dataset("allenai/ai2_arc", "ARC-Challenge", split="test")
    recs = [dict(ds[i]) for i in range(len(ds))]
    ds.to_json("datasets/arc_challenge/test.jsonl")
    save_samples("arc_challenge", recs)
    report["arc_challenge"] = {"n": len(recs), "split": "test", "fields": list(recs[0].keys())}
except Exception as e:
    report["arc_challenge"] = {"error": str(e)}; traceback.print_exc()

# 4. StrategyQA - implicit multi-hop yes/no, requires decomposition
try:
    ds = load_dataset("ChilleD/StrategyQA", split="test")
    recs = [dict(ds[i]) for i in range(len(ds))]
    save_samples("strategyqa", recs)
    with open("datasets/strategyqa/test.jsonl", "w") as f:
        for r in recs: f.write(json.dumps(r, default=str)+"\n")
    report["strategyqa"] = {"n": len(recs), "fields": list(recs[0].keys())}
except Exception as e:
    report["strategyqa"] = {"error": str(e)}; traceback.print_exc()

print("\n=== REPORT ===")
print(json.dumps(report, indent=2))
json.dump(report, open("datasets/_download_report.json", "w"), indent=2)

# Exclude large data files; keep docs and small samples
*
!.gitignore
!README.md
!**/README.md
!**/samples.json
!_download_report.json
EOF
source .venv/bin/activate
git clone --depth 1 https://github.com/lichengliu03/unary-feedback.git code/unary-feedback 2>&1 | tail -3
echo "--- repo contents ---"; ls code/unary-feedback 2>/dev/null | head

content.content:
<system-reminder>[Truncated: PARTIAL view — showing lines 1-73 of 98 total (28287 tokens, cap 25000). Call Read with offset=74 limit=73 for the next page, or Grep to find a specific section. Do NOT answer from this page alone if the answer may be further in the file.]</system-reminder>

1	{
2	  "summary": "Deep-read 7 central papers in parallel, extract structured notes tied to the research hypothesis",
3	  "agentCount": 7,
4	  "logs": [],
5	  "result": [
6	    {
7	      "stem": "emergent_response_planning",
8	      "title": "Emergent Response Planning in LLMs (Dong, Zhou, Liu, Yang, Lu; ICML 2025 / arXiv:2502.06258v3)",
9	      "core_contribution": "The paper shows that LLMs, despite next-token training, encode global attributes of their entire upcoming response in their prompt-level hidden representations BEFORE generating any tokens (\"emergent response planning\"). Using simple MLP probes on prompt representations, they predict structure (response length, reasoning steps), content (story character choice, final multiple-choice answer), and behavior (answer correctness/confidence, factual consistency) attributes of the full response. They provide the first formal definition of response planning and characterize how it scales with model size, evolves across layers, and across the generation timeline.",
10	      "methodology": "Setup: An L-layer decoder LLM pi(y|x). They extract layer-wise hidden representations H^l_x at the prompt position (the position that produces the first response token y1) and train probes h_theta(H^l_x) -> g(y), where g(y) is an \"attribute rule\" summarizing the full greedy-decoded response (e.g., token count). If probing is non-trivially accurate, the model \"planned\" that attribute pre-generation.\n\nSix tasks across three attribute categories, each paired with 2 datasets:\n- Structure: response length (UltraChat, AlpacaEval; regression on token count); reasoning steps (GSM8K, MATH; regression on number of \"Step k:\" markers, capped at 8).\n- Content: character choices (TinyStories, ROCStories; 4-class classification of the animal introduced, excluding animals in first two words); multiple-choice answers (CommonsenseQA, SocialIQA; 5-class A-E, model must give analysis FIRST then answer).\n- Behavior: answer confidence = correctness (MedMCQA, ARC-Challenge; binary correct/incorrect vs ground truth); factual consistency (CREAK, FEVER; binary, match between model's agree/disagree stance and statement truth).\n\nProbes: one-hidden-layer MLPs, ReLU, hidden size grid {1,2,4,8,16,32,64,128,256,512,1024}; 400 epochs; MSELoss (regression), CrossEntropyLoss (classification); 60/20/20 train/val/test; grid search over hidden size AND layer; best-hyperparameter test scores; averaged over 3 seeds. Classes strictly balanced so accuracy = F1.\n\nSpurious-correlation control (\"first-token shortcut\"): prompts engineered so the target attribute is NOT inferable from the immediate next token, e.g., multiple-choice and confidence tasks force the model to write analysis BEFORE the final answer, so initial tokens do not reveal the choice.\n\nData collection/labeling: greedy-decode responses; base-model outputs delimited by [END OF RESPONSE]; pattern-match to extract labels; data augmentation by truncating responses several tokens before the key info appears and recomputing labels, keeping original+augmented in same split. Representations collected via forward pass at truncation point (all layers).\n\nAblations: (5.1) scaling across Llama-2-chat 7B/13B/70B, Llama-3-Instruct 8B/70B, Qwen2-Instruct 7B/72B, Qwen2.5-Instruct 1.5B/32B/72B on UltraChat+TinyStories; (5.2) probing at equidistant positions along the generation timeline (TinyStories character choice, MedMCQA confidence); (5.3) verbalized self-estimate vs probe (response length via \"[TOKENS]number[/TOKENS]\" prompt; character choice).",
11	      "datasets": "UltraChat and AlpacaEval (response length); GSM8K and MATH (reasoning steps); TinyStories and ROCStories (character choices, 4-class top-4 animals); CommonsenseQA and SocialIQA (multiple-choice answers, 5-class); MedMCQA and ARC-Challenge (answer confidence/correctness, binary); CREAK and FEVER (factual consistency, binary). Response length capped at 1000 tokens, reasoning steps capped at 8. All datasets via HuggingFace (links in Appendix A.2). Class-balanced after augmentation. Exact sizes N not stated numerically.",
12	      "baselines": "Random baseline (dashed line) for classification F1 (0.5 for binary confidence/factual-consistency, 0.25 for 4-class character, 0.20 for 5-class MC). For verbalized comparison: model's own self-estimate via direct prompting (Section 5.3) used as a baseline against probing. Base models vs instruction-tuned models compared throughout as the central contrast. No external probing-method baseline; the conceptual baselines are Future Lens (Pal et al. 2023), Geva et al. 2023, Men et al. 2024, Wu et al. 2024 (cited, not re-run).",
13	      "metrics": "Regression: Spearman (S), Kendall (K), Pearson (P) correlation coefficients between predicted and true attribute. Classification: F1 score (= accuracy due to strict class balance). Layer-wise analysis uses absolute + row-normalized performance heatmaps. Verbalization gap measured as Spearman/F1 difference between Self-Estimate and Probing.",
14	      "key_results": "In-dataset probing (Fig 2): response length Qwen2-7B-Instruct K=0.66/S=0.85/P=0.85; reasoning steps Qwen2-7B-Instruct K=0.67/S=0.82/P=0.79. Instruction-tuned > base on structure attributes. Base models weaker but positive on length (e.g., Qwen2-7B K=0.41/S=0.57; Llama-3-8B base K=0.29/S=0.41 — weakest). Content/behavior classification clearly above random for BOTH base and tuned models.\n\nCross-dataset (Fig 3, 10): correlations drop but stay strong (e.g., UltraChat->AlpacaEval length Qwen2-7B-Instruct K=0.46/S=0.64; base Qwen2-7B drops to K=0.20/S=0.30; Llama-2-7B base K=0.15/S=0.23). GSM8K->MATH reasoning steps Qwen2-7B-Instruct K=0.51/S=0.66.\n\nProbe saliency (Fig 4): performance plateaus by hidden size <=128; behavior attributes plateau ~8, structure ~16, content ~32 — a complexity hierarchy.\n\nLayer dynamics (Fig 5): structure attributes peak in MIDDLE layers then partially diminish; content attributes peak in LATER layers; behavior attributes are uniform/stable from early layers.\n\nScaling (Fig 6): within a family, bigger = better planning (length slope 0.042 Spearman, character slope 0.019 F1 across 1.5B-72B), but does NOT generalize across families.\n\nGeneration-timeline dynamics (Fig 7): three-phase U-shape — HIGH accuracy at start (global plan), DECLINE in middle (local token focus), RECOVERY near end. (MedMCQA confidence F1 ~0.70 at start -> ~0.55 mid -> ~0.70 end; TinyStories character ~0.80 -> ~0.65 -> ~0.85.)\n\nVerbalization gap (Fig 8): probing >> self-estimate. Response length probing Spearman ~0.80-0.85 vs self-estimate 0.15-0.49 for tuned models; base models near or below random (Llama-2-7B -0.03, Llama-3-8B -0.04). Character choice probing F1 ~0.72-0.86 vs self-estimate 0.07-0.31. Models encode more than they can verbalize.",
15	      "relevance_to_hypothesis": "Strongly relevant and partly supportive of the hypothesis that models pre-plan their answer/turn-ending. Direct evidence the model commits to global response properties (including final answer, character, and response LENGTH) from the prompt representation before generating, i.e. pre-planning of answer AND of turn length. This is the mechanistic substrate the hypothesis posits would reduce reactivity.\n\nBUT: (1) The paper compares INSTRUCTION-TUNED vs BASE models, NOT RL/RLHF specifically vs base; it provides no RL-vs-base contrast on reactivity. It finds tuned > base on planning STRUCTURE attributes (length, steps), with content/behavior planning present even in base models (attributed to pre-training). This is suggestive that fine-tuning strengthens length/structure commitment — partially consistent with \"RL-tuned models pre-plan more\" — but RLHF is not isolated.\n(2) The three-phase timeline result (Fig 7) is the key nuance for \"reactivity\": planning is strongest at the start (committed plan), weakens mid-generation, and re-strengthens at the end. The early-strong \"global planning intent\" supports the idea of a committed end-of-turn plan; the mid-decline could be read as a window of local flexibility.\n(3) The paper does NOT test reaction to NEW information / feedback mid-stream, and does NOT test rotating certainty across question parts or distributing uncertainty — these are gaps the hypothesis could fill. The \"answer confidence/correctness\" probe shows the model encodes whether it will be right/wrong before answering, which is adjacent to \"uncertainty across question parts.\"\n(4) Greedy decoding only; they explicitly flag distributional/uncertainty-aware probing as open future work (Section 6.1), and propose \"planning-aware training\" and consistency losses (6.4) — directly adjacent to the hypothesis.",
16	      "code_links": "No GitHub repo for the paper's own code is given. Data/model links (HuggingFace) listed in Appendix A.1/A.2: models meta-llama/Llama-2-7b(-chat)-hf, Llama-2-13b/70b-chat-hf, Meta-Llama-3-8B(/-70B-Instruct), mistralai/Mistral-7B-v0.1 and Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.2, Qwen/Qwen2-7B(-Instruct), Qwen2-72B-Instruct, Qwen/Qwen2.5-1.5B/32B/72B-Instruct. Datasets: hf datasets stingning/ultrachat, tatsu-lab/alpaca, openai/gsm8k, deepmind/math_dataset, roneneldan/TinyStories, Ximing/ROCStories, tau/commonsense_qa, allenai/social_i_qa, openlifescienceai/medmcqa, allenai/ai2_arc, amydeng2000/CREAK, fever/fever. (Note: a likely typo — Llama-3-8B-Instruct row links to Llama-2-7b-hf.)",
17	      "reusable_for_experiment": "Highly reusable. PROBING TECHNIQUE: train small MLP probes (hidden grid up to 128, 400 epochs, per-layer grid search, 3 seeds, 60/20/20 split) on prompt/representation activations to predict full-response attributes — directly transferable to test pre-commitment vs reactivity. METRICS: Spearman/Kendall/Pearson for continuous attributes; balanced F1/accuracy for categorical answers; the Self-Estimate-vs-Probing gap protocol. DATASETS for an answer-pre-planning / uncertainty experiment: CommonsenseQA & SocialIQA (5-way MC with forced analysis-before-answer — ideal for probing committed answer before reasoning finishes), MedMCQA & ARC-Challenge (binary correctness/confidence probing — can probe whether the model \"knows the answer it wants to give\"), CREAK & FEVER (stance/factual consistency). The forced \"analysis then answer\" prompt templates (Appendix A.3.1, Tasks 4-5) are reusable to block first-token shortcuts and to create a setting where new info arrives mid-turn. The generation-timeline probing protocol (probe at equidistant truncation points; Fig 7) is directly reusable to measure how/when commitment hardens and whether RL models harden earlier than base. The truncation-based data augmentation method is reusable. To test the rotating-certainty/uncertainty-distribution idea, one could adapt the answer-confidence probe across multi-part questions and use the proposed (unimplemented) distributional probing (predict label distributions, average over ~10 samples) from Section 6.1."
18	    },
19	    {
20	      "stem": "reasoning_strength_planning",
21	      "title": "On Reasoning Strength Planning in Large Reasoning Models (NeurIPS 2025; arXiv:2506.08390v2)",
22	      "core_contribution": "The paper shows that large reasoning models (LRMs) \"pre-plan\" how much reasoning they will do BEFORE generating a single reasoning token: the number of reasoning tokens is linearly predictable from the question's activation at the start-of-reasoning <think> position (Spearman R > 0.8). It further shows this plan is encoded as a single \"pre-allocated direction vector\" in activation space whose magnitude (L2 norm) grows with question difficulty and causally controls reasoning length via activation steering. The vector controls length largely by modulating the logit of the end-of-reasoning </think> token.",
23	      "methodology": "Two probes of a \"pre-planning\" hypothesis. (1) Linear probing for reasoning length: extract the d-dim residual-stream activation h^(l) at the last token position = the start-of-reasoning <think> token (before any reasoning is generated), at each layer l. Fit Lasso linear regression (scikit-learn, regularization alpha=10) to predict y = number of tokens between <think> and </think>. 9:1 train/test split on MATH. Report Spearman R per layer. (2) Difference-in-means direction extraction: compute r_{i<-1}^(l) = mean activation of difficulty-level-i questions minus mean activation of difficulty-level-1 questions, for i=2..5, at the <think> position, per layer. Analyze pairwise cosine similarity of the four vectors and their L2 norms vs difficulty. (3) Causal activation steering: h'^(l) = h^(l) + lambda * r^(l), where r^(l) is the mean of the four difference vectors; sweep lambda from -0.2 to +0.2 in 0.05 steps; applied at each layer and each token position. Measure reasoning token count, answer token count, and accuracy. (4) Link probe to direction: feed r^(l) through the trained predictor (yhat = r W + b) to show the vector yields positive predicted length. (5) Mechanism: measure shift in logit of </think> under steering, compared against the EOS token <|endoftext|> and 500 randomly selected tokens; also measure logits of reflection tokens (Wait, Hmm, Oh, Alright). Control experiment: directly scale </think> logit by factor gamma (0.8, 1, 2) to show naive logit editing is unstable. Generation setup: vLLM, temperature 0.6, max new tokens 16,384, 8 rollouts averaged; prompt = \"Please reason step by step, and put your final answer within \\boxed{}.\" 8 A100 GPUs, ~80 A100 GPU-days. Applications: (a) overthink detection — predict length before generation on 100 AlpacaEval vanilla questions vs their overthink-attack variants; (b) efficient inference — apply negative steering on easy questions (MMLU 100 single-choice questions, MATH500 Level-1) to cut tokens while preserving accuracy.",
24	      "datasets": "MATH (Hendrycks et al.; 5 difficulty levels) — primary probing/direction dataset, 9:1 split. MATH500 (subset) — steering accuracy eval. AIME 2024 — steering eval. OlympiadBench — steering eval. MMLU (100 sampled single-choice questions, lm-evaluation-harness setting) — efficient-inference eval. AlpacaEval (100 sampled vanilla questions + overthink-attack variants) — overthink detection. GPQA diamond — generalization steering. LiveCodeBench (Code Execution and Code Generation) — generalization steering. Overthink attack from Kumar et al. (arXiv 2502.02542) used to construct overthinking variants.",
25	      "baselines": "No competing method baselines (mechanistic study). Internal comparisons/controls: (a) probe correlation across layers and model sizes; (b) impact on </think> logit vs EOS token and 500 random tokens; (c) steering at lambda=0 (no steering) as the reference for accuracy tables; (d) direct logit-scaling by gamma (0.8/1/2) as an alternative to steering, shown to be unstable. Reasoning-length steering directions from prior work (ThinkEdit, SEAL, representation engineering) cited as related but those derive directions from full CoT answers, not from pre-generation question activations.",
26	      "metrics": "Spearman correlation R between predicted and actual reasoning-token count (probe quality). Cosine similarity between difference-in-means direction vectors. L2 norm of direction vectors (vs difficulty). Reasoning token number, answer token number (post-</think>). Accuracy (%) on math/code/QA benchmarks. Logit value of </think> / EOS / random / reflection tokens under steering.",
27	      "key_results": "Probe: Spearman R > 0.8 across model sizes; reported 0.8421 on R1-Distill-Qwen (MATH, Figure 1a); R1-Distill-Llama-8B middle layers R ~ 0.842-0.848. Prediction accuracy increases with layer depth, plateauing in later layers. Direction vectors: pairwise cosine similarity ~0.99 (near 1.0 in final layers), indicating a single shared direction; L2 norm increases monotonically with difficulty, ~proportional to extra reasoning tokens needed. Steering accuracy gains (lambda positive, Table 1, avg over MATH500/AIME2024/OlympiadBench): R1-Distill-Qwen-1.5B 51.84->52.42 (+0.58); 7B 66.91->68.82 (+1.91, AIME +4.58); 14B 72.37->74.72 (+2.35, AIME +5.83); 32B 73.75->75.43 (+1.68); QwQ-32B 64.19->64.65 (+0.46). Negative steering monotonically reduces reasoning tokens and accuracy while leaving answer-token count roughly unchanged. Mechanism: negative steering raises </think> logit (more likely to stop -> fewer tokens), positive steering lowers it; effect on </think> far exceeds EOS and random tokens. Reflection-token logits (Wait, Hmm, Oh, Alright) rise with positive steering. Naive logit scaling gamma=0.8 collapses (accuracy ~0, answer tokens=2, runaway reasoning ~16k tokens), showing logit shift is a result, not cause, of planning. Case study (R1-Distill-Qwen-32B, 1-2+3-...-100): same correct answer -50 but 2253 tokens unsteered vs 113 tokens steered. GPQA diamond and LiveCodeBench show token count tracks lambda and proper positive steering can improve accuracy (e.g., 32B GPQA 55.76->59.77).",
28	      "relevance_to_hypothesis": "STRONGLY supportive of the \"pre-planning\" premise of our hypothesis. The paper provides direct activation-level evidence that LRMs commit to a response-length budget (reasoning strength) BEFORE generating any reasoning token — exactly the \"pre-plan the end of their next turn\" mechanism in our hypothesis. The plan is concretely realized as a single linear direction whose magnitude scales with anticipated difficulty and causally sets where the model will emit </think>. Key nuances for our hypothesis: (1) All studied models are RL/distillation-trained LRMs (DeepSeek-R1 distills, QwQ-32B trained via RL); the paper does NOT compare against non-reasoning base LLMs, so it does not directly test the RL-vs-base differential our hypothesis needs — this is a gap we would fill. (2) The plan is set from the QUESTION activation alone, implying commitment to length before seeing how reasoning unfolds; this is consistent with reduced reactivity to information discovered mid-reasoning. (3) The L2-norm/difficulty coupling means the model maps a single scalar (difficulty/uncertainty) onto a global length budget rather than distributing uncertainty across question parts — relevant to our idea of rotating certainty across sub-questions. (4) The paper frames \"overthinking\" on easy questions as MIS-allocated pre-planned strength, i.e., the pre-plan can be wrong and is not corrected during generation — indirect evidence of failure to revise. It does NOT study multi-turn reactivity or answer pre-planning under new information, leaving our specific reactivity claim untested.",
29	      "code_links": "Code: https://github.com/AlphaLab-USTC/LRM-plans-CoT . Other URLs referenced: model https://huggingface.co/deepseek-ai/DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-7B ; eval https://github.com/EleutherAI/lm-evaluation-harness ; diff-in-means method https://blog.eleuther.ai/diff-in-means/ . Related \"Emergent response planning in LLM\" (Dong et al., arXiv:2502.06258) is the closest cited work on pre-planning answer attributes (confidence, MCQ choice).",
30	      "reusable_for_experiment": "Directly reusable to TEST our hypothesis: (1) Linear-probe-on-<think>-activation technique (Lasso, alpha=10, last-token position, predict y=token count) — adapt to probe whether the ANSWER (not just length) is pre-encoded, and compare RL-tuned LRM vs its base model. (2) Difference-in-means + activation-steering pipeline (h' = h + lambda*r, lambda in [-0.2,0.2]) to causally test commitment/reactivity. (3) </think>-logit-tracking and reflection-token-logit method to detect where/whether the model decides to stop. (4) Datasets/benchmarks: MATH (5 difficulty levels — ideal for \"rotating certainty\"), MATH500, AIME2024, OlympiadBench, GPQA diamond, MMLU, LiveCodeBench, AlpacaEval; overthink-attack construction (Kumar et al. 2502.02542) for stress-testing. (5) Models: full DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen series (1.5B/7B/14B/32B), QwQ-32B, DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Llama-8B — RL/distilled LRMs whose non-reasoning Qwen2.5 / Llama-3 bases are available for the RL-vs-base contrast our hypothesis requires. (6) Generation config (vLLM, temp 0.6, max 16384, 8 rollouts) and \\boxed{} prompt for reproducibility. (7) Closest method to extend for \"answer pre-planning / reactivity\" is the cited Dong et al. 2502.06258 emergent-response-planning probes (pre-planning answer confidence and MCQ choice)."
31	    },
32	    {
33	      "stem": "do_lms_plan_ahead",
34	      "title": "Do Language Models Plan Ahead for Future Tokens? (Wu, Morris, Levine; COLM 2024)",
35	      "core_contribution": "The paper asks whether transformers \"think ahead\" by preparing information at position t for use at future positions t+τ, and distinguishes two mechanisms: \"pre-caching\" (the model deliberately computes features useful for the future at a cost to the present) vs. \"breadcrumbs\" (features useful for the present incidentally also help the future, with no deliberate trade-off). It introduces \"myopic training\" — gradient descent with the off-diagonal (future-loss-to-past-weights) gradient terms removed — and defines a \"myopia gap\" (loss difference between myopic and vanilla models) as a quantitative measure of pre-caching. Finding: a synthetic task forces clear pre-caching, but natural-language models (GPT-2, small Pythia) mostly use breadcrumbs; pre-caching grows with model scale (non-negligible by Pythia 2.8B).",
36	      "methodology": "Theory: For a causal seq-to-seq model with weights tied across positions, total loss gradient is a sum over an upper-triangular Jacobian; off-diagonal terms i<j (gradient of future loss j w.r.t. past weights i) are the training signal that produces pre-caching. \"Myopic descent\" removes these off-diagonal terms (implemented via a \"myopic attention mechanism\" that feeds past key/value states from a cloned-and-detached copy of previous hidden states, so gradients do not flow to past positions). Definitions: untied/tied myopia gap (loss gap between best myopic and best vanilla model), and local myopia bonus (per-model violation of myopia constraints). Theorem 13 proves myopic descent converges to a myopic solution under forward-bias + strong convexity + smoothness. Three model variants implemented with the myopic attention mechanism: (1) myopic-descent model (detached past states), (2) \"local myopic\" model (past key/value states supplied by a separate frozen pretrained vanilla model), (3) \"transformer bigram\" baseline (past key/value states zeroed out). Synthetic experiment (Dp task): regression dataset where y_n = z_n * sum_{i=1}^{a} sin(b*x_{n-i}) + (1-z_n)*x_n, with x~N(0,1), z~Bernoulli(p), a=b=10, N=64. Designed so the model benefits from pre-caching sin(b*x_n) for future use. Two-layer transformer (embd dim 128, 2 heads, n_inner 512, ReLU, AdamW lr 1e-3, Huber loss), trained vanilla vs myopic on 30M length-64 sequences per p in {0.01,0.1,0.3,1}. Linear probes (R^2) and per-neuron correlations fit on hidden states (50,000 eval samples) to detect whether sin(b*x_{n-i}) is computed/stored. Natural language: GPT-2-small (124M; 12 layers, 12 heads, 768 dim, 50257 vocab, CrossEntropy, AdamW lr 6e-4) trained from scratch one epoch on 4.6M MS MARCO sequences truncated to length 64; evaluated per-position on a sliding window over PG-19. Scaling: Pythia suite 14M-2.8B finetuned (from pretrained checkpoints) one epoch on 10M length-64 sequences from The Pile, vanilla vs myopic, evaluated on LAMBADA, PIQA, SciQ, ARC-Easy and validation cross-entropy. Multiplication experiment: GPT-2-small trained on natural-number multiplication (space-delimited digits, reverse digit order, zero-padded), 0/1 exact-match accuracy on 8x8 digit-count grid; filler-token study compares zero-padding to length 5 vs 10. Gradient-angle study: on Pythia-410M training checkpoints, measures norms of myopic vs future gradient components and their cosine similarity over training.",
37	      "datasets": "Synthetic Dp regression task (Dp = D^64_{p,10,10}, 30M training sequences, 50K eval samples). MS MARCO (Nguyen et al. 2016), 4.6M sequences truncated to length 64 (GPT-2 training). PG-19 (Rae et al. 2019), 100K-token sample / sliding window for per-position loss eval. The Pile (Gao et al. 2020), 10M length-64 sequences subsampled for Pythia finetuning. Natural-number multiplication synthetic dataset (10M examples, up to 8 digits; 1024 validation examples per digit-count pair). NLU benchmarks: LAMBADA, PIQA, SciQ, ARC-Easy.",
38	      "baselines": "Vanilla (ordinary) gradient descent model vs. myopic-descent model (the central comparison). \"Local myopic\" model (past hidden states from a separate frozen vanilla model — focuses fully on next-token prediction). \"Transformer bigram\" model (off-diagonal key/value states zeroed; no use of past at all). Trivial constant-zero predictor for the synthetic regression (Huber loss 1.26). On the Dp task, the hand-designed Algorithm 1 (pre-caching) vs Algorithm 2 (brute force, no pre-caching) are conceptual baselines.",
39	      "metrics": "Myopia gap (cross-entropy difference between myopic and vanilla); local myopia bonus; (normalized) Huber loss L/p for regression; linear-probe R^2; per-neuron Pearson correlations; per-position cross-entropy loss; downstream accuracy (LAMBADA/PIQA/SciQ/ARC-Easy); 0/1 exact-match accuracy on multiplication; gradient component norms (myopic vs future) and cosine similarity between them.",
40	      "key_results": "Synthetic Dp: large myopia gap — normalized Huber loss L/p for vanilla vs myopic was 0.096 vs 1.10 (p=0.01), 0.016 vs 0.97 (p=0.1), 0.0030 vs 1.03 (p=0.3), 0.0074 vs 1.26 (p=1.0); trivial baseline = 1.26. Probes confirm layer-1 hidden state encodes sin(b*x_n) and layer-2 encodes the running sum, i.e. clear pre-caching. GPT-2 (MS MARCO): vanilla 3.28, myopic 3.40, local myopic 3.26, transformer bigram 5.33 cross-entropy. So myopia gap = 0.12, local myopia bonus = 0.02 — both small, and far smaller than the 5.33-3.28=2.05 bigram gap, supporting breadcrumbs. Per-position (Fig 4): myopic GPT-2 is BETTER than vanilla at sequence start (it can devote all compute to the next token) but falls behind as context grows; myopia gap rises monotonically with position to ~0.12. Scaling (Pythia): cross-entropy myopia gap grows with size, becoming clearly nonzero from 70M up and non-negligible by 2.8B; vanilla beats myopic on all four benchmarks with the gap widening at larger scale. Multiplication: vanilla > myopic, especially for larger digit counts (e.g., large vanilla-minus-myopic accuracy gaps in the mid/high digit region). Filler tokens: padding multiplicands from length 5 to 10 IMPROVES vanilla accuracy but HURTS myopic accuracy — myopic models cannot exploit filler-token computation. Gradient angles (Pythia-410M): myopic gradient norm consistently exceeds future gradient norm throughout training (training dominated by immediate next-token signal); cosine similarity between myopic and future gradients varies (roughly -0.5 to +0.2) over training.",
41	      "relevance_to_hypothesis": "Strongly relevant as a conceptual/methodological foundation but does NOT directly study RL-fine-tuned models or answer-commitment. Their \"pre-caching\" is exactly the mechanism by which a model could \"pre-plan\" future tokens/answer; the myopia gap quantifies how much a model commits compute now to future outputs. Key takeaways for the hypothesis: (a) Pre-planning (\"pre-caching\") demonstrably exists and INCREASES with model scale — larger models plan further ahead, consistent with the intuition that more capable (and RL-tuned) models commit to answers earlier. (b) The \"future-token meta-prediction\" literature they build on (Pal et al. Future Lens, Hernandez et al., logit/tuned lens) shows future outputs are linearly decodable from a single hidden state — a directly usable probing approach to test whether an RL model has already \"decided\" its answer before generating it. (c) The myopia gap by position result shows models commit increasing future-relevant computation as the sequence lengthens, which could be reframed as reduced reactivity to late-arriving information. Caveats: the paper uses only base / pretrained / lightly-finetuned models (GPT-2, Pythia) — no RLHF/RL models — and does not test reaction to new information, answer revision, or uncertainty across question parts. So it informs (a) and the mechanism of pre-planning but provides no direct evidence on RL-vs-base differences, reactivity, or distributing uncertainty across question parts.",
42	      "code_links": "https://github.com/wiwu2390/FutureGPT2-public (paper: arXiv:2404.00859v2)",
43	      "reusable_for_experiment": "Reusable methods: (1) The \"myopic attention mechanism\" / myopic-training scheme and the myopia gap and local myopia bonus metrics — could be applied to RL-fine-tuned vs base models to measure whether RL increases pre-caching/pre-planning. (2) The \"local myopic\" model construction (swap in frozen past states) as a counterfactual probe of how much a forward pass relies on pre-cached info. (3) Linear probing of hidden states to predict FUTURE tokens (R^2 / correlation methodology; cf. Future Lens by Pal et al. and Hernandez et al. linear relation decoding) — directly usable to test if an RL model has pre-committed to an answer token before emitting it. (4) Per-position / per-token loss analysis to measure how commitment-to-future grows with position (proxy for declining reactivity to new context). (5) The filler-token / zero-padding paradigm to test whether models perform hidden lookahead computation. Reusable datasets/benchmarks: LAMBADA, PIQA, SciQ, ARC-Easy (multiple-choice QA, suitable for answer-commitment probing); MS MARCO, The Pile, PG-19 for LM-loss analysis; the multiplication exact-match task and synthetic Dp generator for controlled pre-caching experiments. Model suites: Pythia 14M-2.8B with public training checkpoints (good for scale studies and gradient-angle analysis); GPT-2-small. For the specific hypothesis we would need to ADD RLHF/RL-tuned model variants and design multi-part / new-information-injection question sets, which the paper does not provide."

46	      "stem": "flipflop_are_you_sure",
47	      "title": "Are You Sure? Challenging LLMs Leads to Performance Drops in The FlipFlop Experiment (Laban, Murakhovs'ka, Xiong, Wu; Salesforce AI Research, 2024)",
48	      "core_contribution": "The paper introduces the FlipFlop experiment, a two-to-three-turn simulated conversation that quantifies LLM sycophancy on classification tasks: round 1 the model answers, round 2 a \"challenger\" utterance (e.g. \"Are you sure?\") invites reconsideration, and the model either confirms or flips. Across 10 LLMs, 7 tasks, and 5 challengers (67,640 conversations), models flip answers 46% of the time and lose 17% accuracy on average between initial and final prediction (the \"FlipFlop effect\"). Finetuning Mistral-7b on balanced synthetic challenge data reduces but does not eliminate the effect (60% reduction).",
49	      "methodology": "7-step procedure (Fig 2): (1) user sends zero-shot classification prompt; (2) LLM gives initial answer via greedy decoding, max 200 tokens (median 10 words / mean 29); (3) user sends challenger utterance; (4) LLM responds (median 36 / mean 67 words); (5) rule-based label extraction from both turns — if model answers affirmatively, initial=final; (6) if extraction fails (rambling), a 3rd confirmation turn \"OK. What is your final answer to the initial question?\" is added; (7) confirmation response (median 27 / mean 46 words). Label extraction succeeds in 95+% of initial responses, only 82% of challenger responses, raised to 97% with the confirmation turn. Uses a \"generate+extract\" method (free-form generation + rule-based label extraction) rather than the \"logprob\" method used in MMLU, arguing it better simulates conversational behavior. Temperature=0 (greedy) for the core experiment; an appendix temperature sweep (GPT-3.5-Turbo on SciQ, T=0.0 to 2.0 in 0.2 steps) shows both initial accuracy degrades and FlipFlop effect magnitude grows ~0.3 per 0.1 temperature increase, so T=0 is a conservative (lower-bound) setting. Challengers must be \"confirmatory\" (an affirmative \"Yes\" should confirm) and are designed to encourage reflection, not coerce. Metrics: Acc_init, Acc_final, FlipFlop effect (Delta_FF = Acc_final - Acc_init), FLIP indicator, flip probabilities Any->Flip, Correct->Flip, Wrong->Flip, and %Sorry (apologetic-keyword presence). (Model,task) pairs where initial accuracy is <5% above random are excluded (performance-based selection). Finetuning (Sec 6): generate 10,000 synthetic non-sycophantic FlipFlop conversations balanced so correct-flipping occurs in 50% of cases; token masking so model learns only from the last assistant response; QLoRA, 1 epoch, batch size 8, single A100. Five variants A-E vary #tasks (1 vs 3), #challengers (1 vs 40), inclusion of Dolly instruction data, and model-specific error filtering.",
50	      "datasets": "Seven evaluation classification tasks (sample counts): Logical Fallacy from BIG-bench (100 samples, Valid/Invalid, 50 each); TruthfulQA (Lin et al. 2022, 400 samples, multiple-choice, 38 categories); New Yorker Captions (Hessel et al. 2023, 100 samples, 4-choice humor); ARC-Challenge (Clark et al. 2018, 400 samples, Challenge subset); SummEdits (Laban et al. 2023, 100 samples, consistent/inconsistent summary factuality, 5+5 across 10 domains); SciQ (Welbl 2017, 100 samples, multiple-choice science, no context paragraph); LegalBench-CCQA / Consumer Contracts QA (Guha et al. 2023, 100 samples, Yes/No). Finetuning synthetic data built from CSQA (Saha et al. 2018), BoolQ (Clark et al. 2019), Logical Fallacy, plus Dolly (Conover et al. 2023) instruction data. Models: 3 open-source — Llama2-7b (meta-llama/Llama-2-7b-chat-hf), Llama2-13b (Llama-2-13b-chat-hf), Mistral-7b (mistralai/Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.1); 7 proprietary — Cohere Command-XL (command-xlarge), Claude V1.3 (claude-v1.3), Claude V2 (claude-2), PaLM2-Bison (text-bison@001), Gemini-Pro, GPT-3.5-Turbo (gpt-3.5-turbo), GPT-4 (gpt-4). API runs Oct 1 - Nov 10, 2023.",
51	      "baselines": "Random-prediction baseline per task (mean 39.3% across tasks). The base (non-finetuned) Mistral-7b-Instruct is the baseline for finetuning experiments. The 10 models are compared against each other; PaLM-Bison and GPT-4 act as low-sycophancy reference points. Comparison drawn to prior sycophancy work (Perez et al. 2022; Wei et al. 2023; Sharma et al. 2023) and self-correction work (Huang et al. 2023a).",
52	      "metrics": "Initial accuracy (Acc_init), final accuracy (Acc_final), FlipFlop effect Delta_FF = Acc_final - Acc_init; flip rates Any->Flip = P(FLIP), Correct->Flip = P(FLIP | initially correct), Wrong->Flip = P(FLIP | initially wrong); %Sorry (apology keyword rate); distribution buckets (major drop <=-10, minor drop (-10,-2], no change (-2,2], minor gain (2,10], major gain >=10); directional flip rate (positive->negative label).",
53	      "key_results": "Across all models: average flip rate 46%, average Delta_FF = -17%. Per-model Delta_FF (Table 2): Claude V1.3 -35.1, Claude V2 -24.8, Gemini-Pro -21.6, GPT-3.5-Turbo -19.7, Llama2-13b -16.8, Mistral-7b -14.5, Llama2-7b -13.7, GPT-4 -6.4, PaLM-bison -5.5, Command-XL -1.3. Flip rates highest for Llama2-7b (69.4%), lowest PaLM-bison (10.3%), GPT-4 (12.8%), Command-XL (14.7%). For ALL models Correct->Flip < Wrong->Flip (interpreted as more uncertainty when initially wrong). Delta_FF strongly correlated with flip rate (rho ~ -0.78) but only moderately with Acc_init (rho = 0.52). Challenger ranking by harm: IDTS -22.9, TEACH -22.8, PHD -17.9, AUS -8.1, ABS -7.2 (most-harmful ~3x least-harmful; persona challengers among top 3). Task ranking: LegalBench-CCQA -30.1 (worst, ~8x), SciQ -19.4, SummEdits -15.7, ARC-C -15.7, NY Captions -12.2, TruthfulQA -9.9, Logical Fallacy -4.9. 73% of experiments show minor/major deterioration; only PaLM-bison and GPT-4 show no change in a majority. Directional flips (Table 3): models show imbalanced flipping — on SummEdits 8/10 models preferentially flip consistent->inconsistent (cautious labeling). Finetuning (Table 4): base Mistral Delta_FF -9.5 (all tasks) / -18.5 (hard subset); best overall Mistral-7b-D (3 tasks, 40 challengers, +Dolly) -3.8 (60% reduction), hard subset -12 to -13; single-task variants A/B made it worse (-17 to -19). %Sorry dropped from base 60% to <2% for all finetuned variants. Error filtering (E) gave no significant benefit, contradicting Wei et al. 2023. Acc_init preserved (~60-64%) after finetuning.",
54	      "relevance_to_hypothesis": "Partial/indirect support. The paper directly measures LLM reactivity to new information (the challenger), which is the core of the hypothesis's \"ability to react.\" However, the framing is inverted: here the failure mode is OVER-reacting (flipping correct answers) rather than under-reacting / pre-committing. It does NOT investigate internal pre-planning of answer or response-length before generation, nor probe hidden states — there is no mechanistic evidence about committing to an answer. On RL vs base: the paper notes RLHF is hypothesized as a sycophancy source (Perez 2022) but FINDS sycophancy is universal across models trained with and without RLHF, and that some models share little instruction/multi-turn data, explicitly questioning whether it also originates in pre-training — this WEAKENS a simple \"RL-finetuning causes the deficit\" claim. Notably the most sycophantic models are Claude V1.3/V2 and GPT-3.5 while GPT-4/PaLM-Bison resist, so heavy RLHF does not monotonically predict the effect. On uncertainty across answer parts: the Correct->Flip < Wrong->Flip result and directional/imbalanced flipping (Table 3) show models hold differential, label-dependent uncertainty — tangentially relevant to \"rotating what is certain across a question.\" The paper does NOT test rotating certainty across a series of questions. Overall it provides a strong reactivity testbed and baseline behavior but no direct evidence on pre-planning/commitment.",
55	      "code_links": "Authors state \"We plan to release code, and data upon acceptance\" (footnote 2) — no concrete repo URL in the paper. Only third-party links present: HuggingFace Hub model IDs, github.com/anthropics/anthropic-sdk-python, docs.cohere.com/docs/the-cohere-platform, github.com/openai/openai-python, newyorker.com/cartoons/contest. arXiv:2311.08596v2.",
56	      "reusable_for_experiment": "Highly reusable: (1) The FlipFlop two/three-turn protocol itself as a reactivity probe — directly adaptable to test whether RL-finetuned vs base models react to mid-turn new information; (2) the 7 benchmark tasks (TruthfulQA, ARC-Challenge, SciQ, SummEdits, LegalBench-CCQA, BIG-bench Logical Fallacy, New Yorker Captions) with stated sample sizes (100-400) and short names; (3) the 5 core challengers (AUS, IDTS, ABS, TEACH, PHD) plus the full 40-challenger list in Appendix A.6 for synthetic data; (4) metrics: Delta_FF, Any/Correct/Wrong->Flip, %Sorry, directional positive->negative flip rate, the 5-bucket distribution — all reusable to quantify reactivity and to compare base vs RLHF models; (5) the generate+extract vs logprob distinction and the temperature-sweep design (T=0..2, step 0.2) to study sensitivity; (6) the balanced synthetic-conversation finetuning recipe (10k convs, 50% correct-flip, token-masked last response, QLoRA) as a mitigation baseline. To test the specific pre-planning hypothesis one would need to ADD hidden-state/answer-token probing and a \"rotating certainty across question parts\" manipulation, which this paper does not provide. The Correct->Flip vs Wrong->Flip metric is a ready proxy for whether a model's commitment depends on its internal certainty."

59	      "stem": "try_again_multiturn",
60	      "title": "A Simple \"Try Again\" Can Elicit Multi-turn LLM Reasoning (Unary Feedback as Observation, UFO)",
61	      "core_contribution": "The paper identifies that single-turn RLVR training (PPO, GRPO, DAPO, Dr. GRPO) collapses a model's multi-turn revision ability: RL-tuned models tend to repeat the exact same answer across turns instead of reacting to feedback. It introduces UFO (Unary Feedback as Observation), a method that converts static single-turn datasets into multi-turn RL episodes by injecting minimal negative feedback (\"Try Again\") after wrong answers, plus a turn-wise reward decay and answer-repetition penalty. UFO improves multi-turn success by up to 14% while preserving single-turn performance.",
62	      "methodology": "Multi-turn problem solving is framed as a finite-horizon MDP (S, A, P, R, T_max). State s_t = Concat(question q, history of {(answer_k, feedback_k)}). Feedback is restricted to NEGATIVE-only unary signals (e.g., \"Try Again\"); a correct answer terminates the episode, so no positive confirmation is ever added to history. Reward r_t = 1 if correct else 0. Training uses PPO (learned critic, GAE with gamma=lambda=1.0, Adam beta=(0.9,0.999), entropy coef 1e-3). Reward design for \"adaptive reasoning\": (1) reward decay R_t = gamma^t (gamma=0.5) to encourage MINIMALITY (fewer turns); (2) repetition penalty Penalty(tau)=lambda*(1 - E(tau)/T) based on number of EFFECTIVE (unique, never-before-seen) answers E(tau) over T turns, to encourage DIVERSITY; final R = R_t - Penalty(tau); small format penalty eta<0 for malformed output. Three reward schedules compared: exponential decay (gamma^n, gamma=0.5), linear decay (max(0,1-0.2(n-1))), constant (=1). Theory: Section 2.3 + Appendix B/C argue single-turn RL produces a peaked, low-entropy distribution; collision probability Coll(q)=sum q(y|x)^2 >= exp(-H(q)) (Jensen), so lower entropy forces higher repetition for a STATIC policy. Defines Parallel Policies (Pi_par, single-turn, sample i.i.d. ignoring feedback) vs Sequential Policies (Pi_seq, condition on full history); Pi_par subset of Pi_seq so max Succ@k(seq) >= max Succ@k(par) (Blackwell dominance); sequential policy can maintain rejection set H_t and approximate sampling-without-replacement, giving conditional success p'_t = p/(1 - sum p_i) > p. Training: Qwen-2.5-3B-Instruct main model, PPO 200 optimization steps on A100, batch P=8 prompts x N=16 rollouts, T_max in {1,5,10}, validation T_max=5, episodes capped at 10 total actions. Prompt uses explicit <think>/<answer> tags, action budget \"You have Y actions left\", max length Z. A separate \"tutor\" model can also provide richer hints (Box 2). Case studies (Boxes 4-7) qualitatively trace pre-RL (adaptive but incomplete), post-single-turn-RL (identical repeated reasoning/answer), UFO success (incremental self-correction), and UFO \"reasoning drift\" (right answer via invalid steps).",
63	      "datasets": "Primary training: MMQ-Math = the MATH subset of MetaMathQA (Yu et al. 2024), augmented from MATH (Hendrycks et al. 2021). Also trained on HotpotQA. Evaluation across 9 datasets in 4 domains: Math (MMQ-Math, TheoremQA [Chen 2023], GSM8K [Cobbe 2021]); STEM (GPQA [Rein 2023], MMLU-STEM [Hendrycks 2020, 15 STEM subjects]); QA (HotpotQA [multi-hop Wikipedia], ConcurrentQA [Arora 2022, temporal/causal]); General (MMLU [57 subjects], MMLU-Pro [Wang 2024b]). For repetition analysis, off-the-shelf RL models from HuggingFace (Table 2): DAPO-Qwen-32B, Qwen2.5-Math-7B/1.5B-Oat-Zero (Dr. GRPO), Qwen-2.5-{0.5B,1.5B,7B,14B}-SimpleRL-Zoo (GRPO), Qwen2.5-7B-PPO-Zero, and their base Qwen models. Released model: LichengLiu03/Qwen2.5-3B-UFO.",
64	      "baselines": "Single-turn PPO-trained model with PARALLEL sampling (k independent completions, evaluated by Pass@k) is the main baseline vs UFO's sequential multi-turn (Succ@k). Also: base models without RL (\"Base Model w/o RL\"), single-turn \"RL on MMQ-Math\", and \"RL on HotQA\". Off-the-shelf RL algorithm comparisons for repetition: PPO, GRPO, DAPO, Dr. GRPO at scales 0.5B-32B. Ablations: T_max in {1,5,10}; reward schedules (constant/linear/exponential); feedback present in train+val vs train-only vs none; verbal feedback wording variants.",
65	      "metrics": "Effective answer / unique answer ratio = a response never explored in any prior turn (key diversity/reactivity metric). Pass@k (single-turn baseline). Succ@k = fraction of problems solved within k interaction turns (Succ@1 single-turn, Succ@5 and Succ@10 multi-turn). AvgTurns = mean number of turns to solve. Collision probability Coll(q) and Shannon entropy H(q) (theory). Number of actions/turns per episode (efficiency).",
66	      "key_results": "REPETITION after single-turn RL: in ~70% of failed cases the single-turn RL model gives EXACTLY the same answer across 5 turns (only 1 effective answer). Unique-answer ratio drops after RL across all methods/scales: DAPO 32B 78.0%->65.7%; Dr.GRPO 1.5B 38.0%->15.4% (largest drop); GRPO 0.5B 34.0%->24.3%; PPO 7B 57.6%->51.7%. UFO's own model: PPO+UFO 3B is the only one whose unique-answer ratio INCREASES after training (~84%->~94%). MAIN GAIN: 5-turn UFO improves multi-turn success up to 14% over single-turn RL with comparable inference cost. Table 1 (5-turn success) examples: Qwen2.5-3B MMQ-Math 79.7->88.5; MMLU 66.8->85.2; MMLU-Pro 48.3->60.9; Qwen2.5-7B MMQ-Math 85.1->93.0; TheoremQA 33.6->42.1; Llama3.2-3B MMQ-Math 86.7->92.2. Gains generalize out-of-domain (math, STEM, QA, general); math-trained generalizes better than QA-trained. T_max=5 and T_max=10 both give >6% relative improvement over single-turn; T_max=5 best, 10 no significant extra gain. Multi-turn training helps even at inference k=1 (better single-shot generalization). Providing explicit feedback gives >8% peak improvement; feedback only-in-training still helps (intrinsic gain). Exponential reward decay reduces mean number of actions ~10% with no loss in success. Effective-answer proportion rises from 80% to >90% over training. Robust across feedback wordings (\"Incorrect.\", \"Please try again.\", \"Please think again.\", \"Let's try again.\").",
67	      "relevance_to_hypothesis": "STRONGLY SUPPORTS the hypothesis that RL-fine-tuned LLMs lose reactivity to new information relative to base models. Direct evidence: (1) base/pre-RL models REVISE their answer across turns given feedback, whereas after single-turn RL they REPEAT the identical answer (Figure 1, Case 1 vs Case 2; ~70% identical-answer rate). (2) Unique-answer ratio consistently DROPS after RL across PPO/GRPO/DAPO/Dr.GRPO and scales 0.5B-32B (Figure 3) - the more an RL method sharpens the policy, the worse the reactivity (Dr.GRPO worst). (3) Mechanistic account: RL creates a peaked, low-entropy distribution; collision probability Coll(q) >= exp(-H(q)) means a near-deterministic policy is mathematically forced to repeat - this is the \"committing to an answer\" phenomenon. The qualitative cases show single-turn RL models commit to one interpretation/derivation in Turn 1 and replay it verbatim Turns 2-5, never reacting to \"try again\" - exactly the \"pre-plan / no revision\" failure. Regarding pre-planning the END/LENGTH of a turn: the paper does not probe internal answer-commitment or response-length planning directly (no hidden-state/logit probing of pre-commitment), so it does not test that specific mechanism, but its repetition-collapse evidence is the behavioral signature. On UNCERTAINTY across question parts: not directly addressed; the paper's lever is rotating FEEDBACK across turns rather than rotating what is certain across question parts, but UFO's \"rejection set\" / sampling-without-replacement framing is conceptually adjacent to forcing the model to distribute exploration. The \"reasoning drift\" failure (Case 4: correct final answer via invalid intermediate steps) is relevant evidence that answer-level reward can decouple the committed answer from faithful reasoning.",
68	      "code_links": "https://github.com/lichengliu03/unary-feedback ; project page unary-feedback.github.io ; released model LichengLiu03/Qwen2.5-3B-UFO (HuggingFace). Off-the-shelf models in Table 2 incl. Qwen/Qwen2.5-32B, BytedTsinghua-SIA/DAPO-Qwen-32B, sail/Qwen2.5-Math-{7B,1.5B}-Oat-Zero, hkust-nlp/Qwen-2.5-{0.5B,1.5B,7B,14B}-SimpleRL-Zoo, RLHFlow/Qwen2.5-7B-PPO-Zero.",
69	      "reusable_for_experiment": "REUSABLE METRICS: \"effective answer / unique answer ratio\" (count of never-before-seen answers across N turns) is a clean, directly reusable measure of reactivity/answer-commitment to compare base vs RL models - precisely operationalizes \"less ability to react to new information\". Also Succ@k vs Pass@k gap, AvgTurns, and collision probability / output entropy H(q) as a proxy for how peaked/pre-committed the policy is. REUSABLE PROTOCOL: their multi-turn \"Try Again\" harness (UFO) turns ANY static single-turn QA/math dataset into a multi-turn reactivity probe - feed a wrong-answer-feedback loop and measure whether the model revises; this is a ready-made testbed for our hypothesis. REUSABLE DATASETS/BENCHMARKS: MMQ-Math (MATH subset of MetaMathQA), GSM8K, MATH, TheoremQA, GPQA, MMLU/MMLU-STEM/MMLU-Pro, HotpotQA, ConcurrentQA. REUSABLE MODEL PAIRS for base-vs-RL comparison at matched scale: Qwen2.5-{0.5B,1.5B,7B,14B} vs their SimpleRL-Zoo (GRPO) RL versions; Qwen2.5-Math vs Oat-Zero (Dr.GRPO); Qwen2.5-32B vs DAPO-Qwen-32B; plus their released base Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct vs Qwen2.5-3B-UFO. To TEST the specific \"pre-planning answer/length\" mechanism the paper leaves open, one could add hidden-state/early-token probing for answer commitment on top of their repetition setup. The \"rotate what is certain across question parts\" idea could be implemented as a variant of their multi-turn rollout where each turn fixes a different sub-component, using the same effective-answer metric to measure forced uncertainty distribution."
70	    },

file.content:
{
  "summary": "Deep-read 7 central papers in parallel, extract structured notes tied to the research hypothesis",
  "agentCount": 7,
  "logs": [],
  "result": [
    {
      "stem": "emergent_response_planning",
      "title": "Emergent Response Planning in LLMs (Dong, Zhou, Liu, Yang, Lu; ICML 2025 / arXiv:2502.06258v3)",
      "core_contribution": "The paper shows that LLMs, despite next-token training, encode global attributes of their entire upcoming response in their prompt-level hidden representations BEFORE generating any tokens ("emergent response planning"). Using simple MLP probes on prompt representations, they predict structure (response length, reasoning steps), content (story character choice, final multiple-choice answer), and behavior (answer correctness/confidence, factual consistency) attributes of the full response. They provide the first formal definition of response planning and characterize how it scales with model size, evolves across layers, and across the generation timeline.",
      "methodology": "Setup: An L-layer decoder LLM pi(y|x). They extract layer-wise hidden representations H^l_x at the prompt position (the position that produces the first response token y1) and train probes h_theta(H^l_x) -> g(y), where g(y) is an "attribute rule" summarizing the full greedy-decoded response (e.g., token count). If probing is non-trivially accurate, the model "planned" that attribute pre-generation.

Six tasks across three attribute categories, each paired with 2 datasets:
- Structure: response length (UltraChat, AlpacaEval; regression on token count); reasoning steps (GSM8K, MATH; regression on number of "Step k:" markers, capped at 8).
- Content: character choices (TinyStories, ROCStories; 4-class classification of the animal introduced, excluding animals in first two words); multiple-choice answers (CommonsenseQA, SocialIQA; 5-class A-E, model must give analysis FIRST then answer).
- Behavior: answer confidence = correctness (MedMCQA, ARC-Challenge; binary correct/incorrect vs ground truth); factual consistency (CREAK, FEVER; binary, match between model's agree/disagree stance and statement truth).

Probes: one-hidden-layer MLPs, ReLU, hidden size grid {1,2,4,8,16,32,64,128,256,512,1024}; 400 epochs; MSELoss (regression), CrossEntropyLoss (classification); 60/20/20 train/val/test; grid search over hidden size AND layer; best-hyperparameter test scores; averaged over 3 seeds. Classes strictly balanced so accuracy = F1.

Spurious-correlation control ("first-token shortcut"): prompts engineered so the target attribute is NOT inferable from the immediate next token, e.g., multiple-choice and confidence tasks force the model to write analysis BEFORE the final answer, so initial tokens do not reveal the choice.

Data collection/labeling: greedy-decode responses; base-model outputs delimited by [END OF RESPONSE]; pattern-match to extract labels; data augmentation by truncating responses several tokens before the key info appears and recomputing labels, keeping original+augmented in same split. Representations collected via forward pass at truncation point (all layers).

Ablations: (5.1) scaling across Llama-2-chat 7B/13B/70B, Llama-3-Instruct 8B/70B, Qwen2-Instruct 7B/72B, Qwen2.5-Instruct 1.5B/32B/72B on UltraChat+TinyStories; (5.2) probing at equidistant positions along the generation timeline (TinyStories character choice, MedMCQA confidence); (5.3) verbalized self-estimate vs probe (response length via "[TOKENS]number[/TOKENS]" prompt; character choice).",
      "datasets": "UltraChat and AlpacaEval (response length); GSM8K and MATH (reasoning steps); TinyStories and ROCStories (character choices, 4-class top-4 animals); CommonsenseQA and SocialIQA (multiple-choice answers, 5-class); MedMCQA and ARC-Challenge (answer confidence/correctness, binary); CREAK and FEVER (factual consistency, binary).       "relevance_to_hypothesis": "Strongly relevant and partly supportive of the hypothesis that models pre-plan their answer/turn-ending. This is the mechanistic substrate the hypothesis posits would reduce reactivity.

BUT: (1) The paper compares INSTRUCTION-TUNED vs BASE models, NOT RL/RLHF specifically vs base; it provides no RL-vs-base contrast on reactivity. It finds tuned > base on planning STRUCTURE attributes (length, steps), with content/behavior planning present even in base models (attributed to pre-training). This is suggestive that fine-tuning strengthens length/structure commitment — partially consistent with "RL-tuned models pre-plan more" — but RLHF is not isolated.
(2) The three-phase timeline result (Fig 7) is the key nuance for "reactivity": planning is strongest at the start (committed plan), weakens mid-generation, and re-strengthens at the end. The early-strong "global planning intent" supports the idea of a committed end-of-turn plan; the mid-decline could be read as a window of local flexibility.
(3) The paper does NOT test reaction to NEW information / feedback mid-stream, and does NOT test rotating certainty across question parts or distributing uncertainty — these are gaps the hypothesis could fill. The "answer confidence/correctness" probe shows the model encodes whether it will be right/wrong before answering, which is adjacent to "uncertainty across question parts."
